title: Quiz: Scale-up and Manufacturing Modeling
description: Test your understanding of computational scale-up support

questions.question: What does a Damköhler number >> 1 indicate?

options.text: The reaction is too slow to measure

questions.explanation: The Damköhler number Da = reaction rate / mixing rate. When Da >> 1, the reaction is fast compared to mixing, so mixing (transport) controls the overall rate. Lab kinetics may not directly apply at this scale.

questions.question: Why does heat transfer become more challenging at larger scale?

options.text: Surface area to volume ratio decreases

questions.explanation: As vessels scale up, the surface area (for heat transfer) increases with the square of the dimension while volume increases with the cube. This means A/V ratio decreases by ~100x going from lab to production scale, making heat removal much more difficult.

questions.question: What is the primary computational approach for predicting polymorphs?

options.text: Crystal structure prediction with lattice energy ranking

questions.explanation: Crystal structure prediction (CSP) generates candidate crystal packings, then ranks them by lattice energy calculated with dispersion-corrected DFT (DFT-D). The most stable packings are likely polymorphs that may appear experimentally.

questions.question: In multiscale modeling, what information flows from DFT to process simulation?
questions.explanation: DFT provides fundamental rate constants (k), activation energies (Ea), and thermodynamic data (ΔH, ΔG) that are inputs to process simulation software. This information flows 'up' from the molecular scale to the reactor scale.

questions.question: A metastable polymorph is always preferred because it is more soluble.
questions.explanation: False. While metastable polymorphs are often more soluble, they can convert to the stable form during storage or processing, potentially causing product failure (as happened with Ritonavir). The stable form is often preferred for robustness, even if less soluble.

questions.question: What changes significantly when scaling from lab to production? (Select all)

options.text: The molecular mechanism of the reaction

questions.explanation: Surface/volume ratio decreases dramatically with scale, mixing time increases, and heat transfer becomes more difficult. The molecular mechanism (bonds forming/breaking) stays the same - only the transport phenomena change.
